summary: Ancient Greek and Roman philosophical school founded by Zeno of Citium at the 'painted porch' (stoa) in Athens. Core doctrines: virtue is the only good, control of emotions, prohairesis (volition), pneuma, ekpyrosis (great conflagration), and propositional logic. Major figures include Chrysippus, Cleanthes, Epictetus, Seneca, and Marcus Aurelius. Divided philosophy into logic, physics, and ethics.

works.name: Prohairesis, Volition, and Stoic Ethics
works.description: The central ethical concept of Stoicism is prohairesis (volition) -- the only thing fully within our control. Epictetus argued in the Discourses (compiled by Arrian) and the Enchiridion (Handbook/Manual) that one should only be concerned with one's own actions. The Stoics held that virtue is the only good, with health and wealth classified as 'indifferents' having only 'selective value.' The school distinguished between katorthomata (perfect action) and kathekonta (appropriate action). Kathekon (proper action in accordance with nature) was taught from the 'painted porch.'

clues.clue: Prohairesis (volition): the only thing within our control/power
examples: the only thing fully in our power is our own volition, or prohairesis
the only thing 'in our power' is prohairesis, or volition

clues.clue: Epictetus: Discourses (compiled by Arrian) and Enchiridion (Handbook/Manual)
examples: a work compiled by Arrian as the Discourses
the Enchiridion

clues.clue: Virtue is the only good; health and wealth are 'indifferents' with 'selective value'
examples: the primary doctrine of this philosophical school is that virtue is necessary and sufficient for happiness
classified all external aspects of life as 'indifferents'

clues.clue: Distinguished goods from 'preferred'/'dispreferred' things; Rachel Barney's puzzle
examples: Rachel Barney explores this school's approach to things that are 'preferred' or 'dispreferred'

clues.clue: Kathekonta (appropriate action) vs. katorthomata (perfect action)
examples: while ordinary people cannot perform katorthomata, they can perform kathekonta

clues.clue: Oikeiosis: perceiving 'ownership'; Hierocles's concentric circles
examples: Hierocles' account of this process describes a series of concentric circles

clues.clue: Seneca: moral letters to Lucilius; On the Shortness of Life; On Mercy (dedicated to Nero)
examples: advocated treating slaves well in his moral letters to Lucilius and wrote On the Shortness of Life

clues.clue: Epictetus: 'behave in life as at a dinner party'; compared life to an athlete striking a ball
examples: a short book on this school of thought contains the injunction to 'behave in life as at a dinner party'

clues.clue: Marcus Aurelius: Meditations, written during military campaigns; 'either Providence or blind atoms'
examples: the ideal 'sage' is discussed in the Meditations
proposed the alternatives of 'either Providence or blind atoms'

clues.clue: Premeditatio malorum (Seneca): envision negative outcomes ahead of time
examples: 'All the terms of our human lot should be before our eyes'

works.name: Stoic Physics: Pneuma, Ekpyrosis, and Cosmology
works.description: Stoic physics held that the universe is infused with pneuma, a mixture of air and fire serving as a vital organizing substance. The cosmos undergoes cyclical destruction through ekpyrosis (great conflagration) at the end of each 'Great Year.' This doctrine of eternal recurrence was later adopted by Nietzsche. Heraclitus's 'fire everliving' influenced the Stoic belief in conflagration. The Stoics held that only bodies exist and that moral qualities and the soul are corporeal. The logos spermatikos is the supreme creative force.

clues.clue: Pneuma: mixture of air and fire infusing the universe
examples: the universe was infused with a mixture of air and fire called pneuma

clues.clue: Ekpyrosis (great conflagration): cosmos cyclically destroyed by fire
examples: every Great Year ends with an ekpyrosis, or great conflagration, destroying the cosmos

clues.clue: Only bodies exist; moral qualities and soul are corporeal
examples: members of this school argued that only bodies exist, and that moral qualities and the soul are corporeal

clues.clue: Logos spermatikos: supreme creative force
examples: Logos spermatikos is the supreme creative force in this philosophical school

works.name: Stoic Logic
works.description: The Stoics developed the first form of propositional logic, based on propositions rather than Aristotelian terms. Truth-bearers were lekta ('sayables'). Chrysippus created a system with five proposition connectives and was the most prolific Stoic logician. He died laughing (supposedly after seeing a donkey eat figs). His 'growing argument' (Dion thought experiment) addressed identity, inspiring modern work like Wiggins's Tibbles the Cat. The Stoics used a hand analogy for levels of understanding (open palm = perception, covered fist = knowledge). The 'cognitive impression' was the criterion of truth.

clues.clue: Propositional logic: based on propositions, not Aristotelian terms; lekta ('sayables') as truth-bearers
examples: developed a form of logic based on propositions rather than terms
logic did not describe relations between words but between 'sayables' called lekta

clues.clue: Chrysippus: five proposition connectives; died laughing (donkey eating figs); 'growing argument' (Dion)
examples: Five proposition connectives were developed by a follower who literally died of laughter
introduced a paradox of identity with a thought experiment involving Dion

clues.clue: Hand analogy for understanding: open palm = perception, covered fist = knowledge
examples: used a hand to illustrate different levels of understanding, with an open palm being perception and a covered fist being knowledge

clues.clue: Cognitive impression as criterion of truth
examples: the criterion of truth is the 'cognitive impression'
the perfect sage never makes epistemological mistakes because they only assent to cognitive impressions

clues.clue: Chrysippus: dog using disjunctive syllogism; cone/cylinder analogy for determinism
examples: described how his dog used a disjunctive syllogism to decide which path to take
analogy of a cone and cylinder rolling differently after an initial push

works.name: General / School Identity and Reception
works.description: Founded by Zeno of Citium at the 'painted porch' (stoa) in Athens. Divided philosophy into logic, physics, and ethics (compared to an egg with logic on the outside). Pierre Hadot connected the three disciplines to desire, action, and assent. The school's ideal 'sage' was a recurring concept. Justus Lipsius attempted to synthesize Stoicism with Christianity (neostoicism) in On Constancy, but neostoics abandoned the belief in fate. The cognitivist theory of emotions traces back to Stoic understanding of emotions as judgments. Cicero critiqued Stoicism in Pro Murena. The dog-tied-to-cart analogy illustrated determinism and free will.

clues.clue: Founded by Zeno of Citium; taught at the 'painted porch' (stoa poikile)
examples: Zeno of Citium founded this tradition
named from the 'painted porch' where its founder Zeno of Citium taught

clues.clue: Divided philosophy into logic, physics, and ethics (like an egg)
examples: divided philosophy into the three subjects of logic, physics, and ethics
compared philosophy to an egg with logic on the outside

clues.clue: Hadot: three disciplines (desire, action, assent) map to physics, ethics, logic
examples: three disciplines of desire, action, and assent were connected by Pierre Hadot

clues.clue: Justus Lipsius: On Constancy; neostoicism (synthesized with Christianity)
examples: Justus Lipsius attempted to synthesize this ancient philosophy with Christianity

clues.clue: Dog tied to cart analogy for determinism and free will (Cleanthes)
examples: a school of ancient philosophy attempted to harmonize a purely autonomous will with a deterministic model using an analogy involving dogs

clues.clue: Marcus Aurelius: Meditations written during military campaigns; erased false impressions
examples: described striving to live as a philosopher in notes written during a military campaign

clues.clue: Albert Ellis: REBT framework inspired by Stoicism
examples: this school of thought heavily inspired the development of the REBT framework by Albert Ellis

clues.clue: Amor fati (love of fate): Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius inspired Nietzsche
examples: Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius inspired Nietzsche's love, or amor, of fate

comprehensive_summary:
Stoicism is one of the most heavily clued Hellenistic philosophical schools, founded by Zeno of Citium at the 'painted porch' in Athens and including major figures such as Chrysippus, Cleanthes, Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius. The school divided philosophy into logic, physics, and ethics, which Pierre Hadot connected to the disciplines of desire, action, and assent.

The central ethical concept is prohairesis (volition) -- the only thing fully within our control, as taught by Epictetus in his Discourses (compiled by Arrian) and the Enchiridion. Virtue is the only good, with health and wealth classified as 'indifferents.' Marcus Aurelius's Meditations, written during military campaigns, discusses the ideal sage and proposes 'either Providence or blind atoms.' Seneca wrote moral letters to Lucilius, On the Shortness of Life, and On Mercy (dedicated to Nero).

Stoic physics held that the universe is infused with pneuma (a mixture of air and fire) and undergoes cyclical destruction through ekpyrosis (great conflagration). Heraclitus's fire influenced this doctrine. The logos spermatikos is the supreme creative force. Only bodies truly exist in Stoic ontology.

The Stoics developed the first form of propositional logic, using lekta ('sayables') as truth-bearers rather than Aristotelian terms. Chrysippus created five proposition connectives and died laughing at a donkey eating figs. His 'growing argument' about Dion addressed personal identity. He described a dog using disjunctive syllogism and used the cone/cylinder analogy for determinism. The 'cognitive impression' served as the criterion of truth.

Justus Lipsius synthesized Stoicism with Christianity in On Constancy, founding neostoicism. The cognitivist theory of emotions traces to Stoic judgments. Cicero critiqued Stoicism in Pro Murena. The dog-tied-to-cart analogy (Cleanthes) illustrated the harmony of autonomous will with determinism. Albert Ellis's REBT framework was inspired by Stoicism, and Nietzsche's amor fati was inspired by Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius.

cards.front: Concept: the Stoic term for volition, the only thing fully within one's power, central to the philosophy of Epictetus
Work: compiled by Arrian, contains the injunction to 'behave in life as at a dinner party' and establishes three topoi one must follow
Philosopher: wrote moral letters to Lucilius, On the Shortness of Life, and On Mercy (dedicated to a ruler who later ordered his suicide)
Work: 12-book set of notes in which the author proposes 'either Providence or blind atoms' and reminds himself to 'erase' false impressions, partly written at a camp by the river Gran
Concept: Stoic doctrine that the cosmos is cyclically destroyed by fire at the end of each 'Great Year', influenced by Heraclitus's 'fire everliving'
cards.work: Stoic Physics: Pneuma, Ekpyrosis, and Cosmology

cards.front: Concept: a mixture of air and fire that infuses the universe as a vital organizing substance in Stoic cosmology
cards.work: Stoic Physics: Pneuma, Ekpyrosis, and Cosmology

cards.front: Philosopher: most prolific Stoic logician who died laughing after seeing a donkey eat figs; created five proposition connectives; introduced the 'growing argument' about Dion
Philosopher: described how a dog uses disjunctive syllogism to choose which path to take; used cone/cylinder analogy for determinism
Concept: Stoic truth-bearers described as 'sayables,' which the founder said 'were not somethings or qualified things, but rather quasi-somethings'
Philosopher: 16th-century Flemish author of On Constancy, sparked the development of neostoicism by synthesizing this ancient school with Christianity
Concept: Stoic analogy using a dog tied to a cart to illustrate how an autonomous will can be harmonized with determinism
